options.D: One side may be building trust primarily through demonstrated competence and formal process, while the other requires personal rapport and relationship investment before committing.

scenario: A professional from Brazil challenges a colleague from Peru's proposal in a team meeting, listing logical flaws and inviting the group to improve the idea. Peru's colleague responds politely but withdraws from subsequent discussions.
question: What is the most accurate interpretation of the interaction?

options.A: The recipient expected criticism to come privately and softened first, and read the blunt public challenge as a judgement on their overall professional competence.
options.B: A long-standing rivalry between the two colleagues shaped the whole exchange, so the withdrawal followed that ongoing interpersonal history far more than the directness of this meeting.
options.C: The proposal under challenge was one the recipient had little personal ownership of, so the withdrawal reflected a reduced stake rather than any reaction to the manner of the challenge.
options.D: The direct challenger intended open debate as intellectual engagement; the recipient experienced public criticism as a threat to professional standing.

options.A: The recipient expected criticism to come privately and softened first, and read the blunt public challenge as a judgement on their overall professional competence.
options.B: A long-standing rivalry between the two colleagues shaped the whole exchange, so the withdrawal followed that ongoing interpersonal history far more than the directness of this meeting.
options.C: The direct challenger intended open debate as intellectual engagement; the recipient experienced public criticism as a threat to professional standing.
options.D: The proposal under challenge was one the recipient had little personal ownership of, so the withdrawal reflected a reduced stake rather than any reaction to the manner of the challenge.
